title: Plastic waste generation by region and polymer
subtitle: Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).
caption:
Plastic waste generation by region and polymer

Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).

This stacked horizontal bar chart displays the regional composition of plastic waste generation for thirteen polymer types in 2019. The horizontal axis represents the percentage share from 0% to 100%, while the vertical axis lists categories including PVC, PUR, PS, HDPE, PET, and others. Each bar is segmented by color to show the contribution of nine regions: Europe, China, Asia (excluding China and India), Middle East & North Africa, India, Sub-Saharan Africa, Americas (excluding USA), Oceania, and the United States. Regional aggregates were calculated by Our World in Data and are based on those specified by the OECD. The United States, represented by teal segments on the far right, constitutes a significant portion of the total for nearly every polymer type, often occupying the space from roughly 80% to 100%. Europe, shown in blue on the left side of the bars, consistently accounts for approximately 20% to 30% of the waste across most categories. China, depicted in maroon, generally forms the second largest segment, extending from the European share to roughly the 40% mark. Asia (excluding China and India), colored green, typically fills the section between 40% and 55%. The remaining regions, including India, the Middle East & North Africa, and the Americas (excluding USA), appear as narrower bands in the middle-to-right sections of the bars. The visual distribution remains relatively consistent across different polymers, with the United States, Europe, China, and Asia (excluding China and India) comprising the vast majority of the stacked bars for all listed materials.
